nsci-001.quizQuestion: How much hotter is lightning than the Sun's surface?
nsci-001.statement: A bolt of lightning is five times hotter than the surface of the Sun

nsci-002.quizQuestion: More possible chess games or atoms in universe?
nsci-002.statement: There are more possible iterations of a game of chess than there are atoms in the observable universe

nsci-003.quizQuestion: How fast do Neptune's winds travel?
nsci-003.statement: Neptune has winds that travel faster than the speed of sound on Earth

nsci-004.quizQuestion: What unusual precipitation occurs inside Neptune and Uranus?
nsci-004.statement: It rains diamonds on Neptune and Uranus

nsci-005.quizQuestion: How much would a teaspoon of neutron star material weigh?
nsci-005.statement: A neutron star is so dense that a teaspoon of its material would weigh about a billion tonnes on Earth

nsci-006.quizQuestion: What caused Earth's atmosphere to become oxygen-rich?
nsci-006.statement: Oxygen was not always a major component of Earth's atmosphere — it was once almost nonexistent

nsci-007.correctAnswer: Hot water freezes faster than cold
nsci-007.statement: Hot water can freeze faster than cold water under certain conditions — this is called the Mpemba effect

nsci-008.quizQuestion: When did Voyager 1 enter interstellar space?
nsci-008.statement: The Voyager 1 spacecraft, launched in 1977, is now in interstellar space — the first human-made object to leave the solar system

nsci-009.quizQuestion: Why can't sound travel through outer space?
nsci-009.correctAnswer: Space lacks a medium for sound waves
nsci-009.statement: Sound cannot travel through space — space is a near-perfect vacuum

nsci-010.quizQuestion: What was the Sahara Desert like 6,000 years ago?
nsci-010.statement: The Sahara Desert was a lush, green savanna with lakes and rivers only 6,000 years ago

nsci-011.quizQuestion: By approximately how much does water expand when freezing?
nsci-011.statement: Water expands by about 9% when it freezes — which is why ice floats and pipes burst in winter

nsci-012.quizQuestion: Earth's core temperature compares to Sun's surface how?
nsci-012.statement: The centre of the Earth is approximately the same temperature as the surface of the Sun

nsci-013.quizQuestion: Why is a Venus day longer than its year?
nsci-013.statement: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus

nsci-014.statement: Helium was discovered on the Sun before it was found on Earth

nsci-015.quizQuestion: What did Einstein call quantum entanglement?
nsci-015.statement: Quantum entanglement allows two particles to be instantly correlated regardless of the distance between them

nsci-016.quizQuestion: Why are old medieval window panes thicker at bottom?
nsci-016.statement: Glass is technically a supercooled liquid, not a solid — old window panes are thicker at the bottom because glass flows extremely slowly

nsci-017.quizQuestion: How much carbon does average adult contain?
nsci-017.statement: The human body contains enough carbon to make nearly 9,000 pencils

nsci-018.quizQuestion: What is the observable universe's approximate diameter?
nsci-018.statement: The observable universe is about 93 billion light-years in diameter, even though it is only 13.8 billion years old

nsci-019.quizQuestion: What is a zeptosecond in seconds?
nsci-019.statement: The shortest unit of time ever measured is the zeptosecond — about 247 zeptoseconds for light to cross a hydrogen molecule

nsci-020.quizQuestion: What is a rainbow's complete geometric shape?
nsci-020.statement: Rainbows are actually full circles — we only see arcs because the ground cuts them off

lsci-001.quizQuestion: What sugar do tardigrades use for extreme survival?
lsci-001.statement: Tardigrades can survive the vacuum of space, intense radiation, and temperatures from near absolute zero to 150°C

lsci-002.quizQuestion: What makes honey resistant to spoilage?
lsci-002.correctAnswer: Low water, high acidity, hydrogen peroxide
lsci-002.statement: Honey never spoils — archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still edible

lsci-003.quizQuestion: How far would all human body DNA stretch if uncoiled?
lsci-003.statement: If you stretched out all the DNA in a single human body, it would reach from Earth to Pluto and back — multiple times

lsci-004.quizQuestion: What nickname describes underground fungal forest networks?
lsci-004.statement: Trees in a forest communicate and share nutrients through underground fungal networks nicknamed the 'Wood Wide Web'

lsci-005.quizQuestion: How many colour receptors does mantis shrimp have?
lsci-005.statement: The mantis shrimp can punch with the acceleration of a bullet and sees colours humans cannot imagine

lsci-006.quizQuestion: Cleopatra lived closer to Moon landing or Great Pyramid?
lsci-006.statement: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza

lsci-007.quizQuestion: Which existed first on Earth: sharks or trees?
lsci-007.statement: Sharks are older than trees — they existed 200 million years before the first forests appeared on Earth

lsci-008.quizQuestion: What is the collective noun for flamingos?
lsci-008.statement: A group of flamingos is called a flamboyance

lsci-009.quizQuestion: What would realistically happen to Marlin after Nemo's mother died?
lsci-009.statement: Clownfish are all born male — the dominant fish in a group can change sex to become female

lsci-010.quizQuestion: How much does a blue whale's heart weigh?
lsci-010.statement: The heart of a blue whale is so large that a human could crawl through its aorta

lsci-011.quizQuestion: What did crows remember about mask-wearing researchers?
lsci-011.correctAnswer: Remembered faces for years, taught offspring
lsci-011.statement: Crows remember human faces and can hold grudges for years

lsci-012.quizQuestion: Which fruit is botanically classified as true berry?
lsci-012.statement: Bananas are technically berries, but strawberries are not

lsci-013.quizQuestion: What percentage of DNA do humans share with bananas?
lsci-013.statement: Humans share about 60% of their DNA with bananas

lsci-014.quizQuestion: What organs do jellyfish completely lack?
lsci-014.statement: Jellyfish have survived for over 500 million years without a brain, heart, or blood

lsci-015.quizQuestion: How long was a day during dinosaur times?
lsci-015.statement: A day was only 22 hours long when dinosaurs roamed the Earth

lsci-016.quizQuestion: What unique shape do wombat droppings have?
lsci-016.statement: Wombat faeces are cube-shaped — the only known animal to produce cubic droppings

lsci-017.quizQuestion: What function does the appendix serve?
lsci-017.statement: The appendix was once thought useless but is now known to serve as a reservoir for beneficial gut bacteria

lsci-018.quizQuestion: What animal group is the electric eel most closely related to?
lsci-018.statement: The electric eel is not actually an eel — it is more closely related to catfish

lsci-019.correctAnswer: Lack functional sweet taste receptor gene
lsci-019.statement: Cats can't taste sweetness — they completely lack the taste receptor gene for sugar

lsci-020.quizQuestion: Why is octopus blood blue instead of red?
lsci-020.statement: Octopuses have three hearts and blue blood

lsci-021.quizQuestion: What can axolotls regenerate throughout their lives?
lsci-021.statement: Axolotls can regenerate entire limbs, parts of their heart, and portions of their brain

hist-001.quizQuestion: Which came first: Oxford University or the Aztec Empire?
hist-001.statement: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire

hist-002.quizQuestion: How long did the Anglo-Zanzibar War last?
hist-002.statement: The shortest war in recorded history lasted only 38 to 45 minutes — between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896

hist-003.quizQuestion: Can the Great Wall of China be seen from space naked eye?
hist-003.statement: The Great Wall of China is NOT visible from space with the naked eye

hist-004.quizQuestion: What was Napoleon's actual height in modern measurement?
hist-004.statement: Napoleon Bonaparte was not particularly short — he was about average height for his time

hist-005.quizQuestion: How was the Library of Alexandria most likely destroyed?
hist-005.statement: The Library of Alexandria may have been destroyed gradually over centuries — not in a single dramatic fire

hist-006.quizQuestion: What was originally green about Coca-Cola?

hist-007.quizQuestion: What was the first computer bug in 1947?
hist-007.correctAnswer: A moth in Harvard Mark II
hist-007.statement: The first computer bug was a literal bug — a moth found in a relay of the Harvard Mark II in 1947

hist-008.quizQuestion: Why did ancient Romans use urine as toothpaste?
hist-008.statement: Ancient Romans used urine as mouthwash and toothpaste — it was an effective cleaning agent

hist-009.quizQuestion: Where did the Vikings-with-horned-helmets myth originate?
hist-009.statement: The Vikings never wore horned helmets — that image was invented by a 19th-century costume designer

hist-010.quizQuestion: In what year was the guillotine last used in France?
hist-010.statement: The guillotine was last used in France in 1977 — the same year Star Wars was released

hist-011.quizQuestion: How many confirmed deaths from the Great Fire of London, 1666?
hist-011.statement: The Great Fire of London in 1666 killed very few people — possibly only six confirmed deaths

hist-012.quizQuestion: What antimicrobial did ancient Egyptians apply to infected wounds?
hist-012.statement: Ancient Egyptians used moldy bread as an antibiotic treatment for infected wounds

hist-013.quizQuestion: What did Caesar do after pirates ransomed him?
hist-013.statement: Julius Caesar was once kidnapped by pirates, and after his release he returned and had them crucified

hist-014.quizQuestion: How much taller does the Eiffel Tower grow in summer?
hist-014.statement: The Eiffel Tower grows taller in summer — it can increase in height by up to 15 cm due to thermal expansion

hist-015.statement: Ancient Romans had 24-hour fast food restaurants called thermopolia

hist-016.quizQuestion: What happened to Einstein's brain after his 1955 death?
hist-016.statement: Einstein's brain was stolen after his death and kept in a jar for decades

hist-017.quizQuestion: Were Great Pyramid workers enslaved or paid?
hist-017.statement: The construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza took 20 years and about 20,000–30,000 workers — not enslaved people

hist-018.quizQuestion: What is humanity's oldest known written recipe for?
hist-018.statement: The world's oldest known recipe is a Sumerian beer recipe from around 1800 BC

hist-019.quizQuestion: What knotted string system did Incas use for records?
hist-019.statement: The Inca Empire had no writing system but used knotted strings called quipus to record information

hist-020.quizQuestion: How did Black Death labour scarcity help end feudalism?
hist-020.correctAnswer: Peasants gained bargaining power for wages
hist-020.statement: The Black Death killed so many people in Europe that labour became scarce and wages rose — helping end feudalism

hist-021.quizQuestion: What was the 'War of Currents' between Edison and Tesla?
hist-021.correctAnswer: Rivalry over AC versus DC electricity
hist-021.statement: Nikola Tesla and Thomas Edison had a bitter rivalry called the 'War of Currents' over AC versus DC electricity

geo-001.quizQuestion: How many time zones span Russia?
geo-001.statement: Russia is so wide it spans 11 time zones — more than any other country on Earth.

geo-002.quizQuestion: Which structure is falsely claimed visible from space?
geo-002.statement: The Great Wall of China is NOT visible from space with the naked eye — it's a persistent myth.

geo-003.quizQuestion: What percentage of global freshwater discharge comes from Amazon?
geo-003.statement: The Amazon River discharges more freshwater into the ocean than the next seven largest rivers combined.

geo-004.quizQuestion: Which mountain is tallest from base to summit?
geo-004.statement: Mount Everest is the tallest mountain above sea level, but Mauna Kea in Hawaii is the tallest mountain measured from its base.

geo-005.quizQuestion: Which desert is the world's largest hot desert?
geo-005.statement: The Sahara Desert is roughly the same size as the contiguous United States.

geo-006.quizQuestion: What percentage of world's lake water does Canada hold?
geo-006.statement: Canada has more lakes than all other countries combined, holding about 60% of the world's lake water.

geo-007.quizQuestion: Pacific Ocean larger than all Earth's landmasses?
geo-007.statement: The Pacific Ocean is larger than all of Earth's landmasses combined.

geo-008.quizQuestion: What is the world's smallest country by area?
geo-008.statement: Vatican City is the world's smallest country, entirely surrounded by Rome, Italy — and it has its own army.

geo-009.quizQuestion: How much does Dead Sea's water level drop yearly?
geo-009.statement: The Dead Sea is so salty you cannot sink in it — and it gets saltier and smaller every year.

geo-010.quizQuestion: Which desert is the driest non-polar desert on Earth?
geo-010.statement: The Atacama Desert in Chile is the driest non-polar desert on Earth — some parts have NEVER recorded rainfall.

geo-011.quizQuestion: Which country has the most islands?
geo-011.statement: Indonesia is an archipelago of over 17,000 islands — more than any other country in the world.

geo-012.quizQuestion: Which continent lies in all four hemispheres?
geo-012.statement: Africa is the only continent in all four hemispheres — north, south, east, and west.

geo-013.quizQuestion: Which river traditionally holds world's longest title?
geo-013.statement: The Nile and Amazon rivers dispute the title of 'world's longest river,' with measurements varying by how tributaries are counted.

geo-014.quizQuestion: How many people live in Greater Tokyo metropolitan area?
geo-014.statement: Tokyo is the world's largest metropolitan area, home to over 37 million people — more than the entire population of Canada.

geo-015.quizQuestion: Which country has both Sucre and La Paz as capitals?
geo-015.statement: Bolivia has TWO capitals — Sucre (constitutional) and La Paz (seat of government) — the only country with this arrangement.

geo-016.quizQuestion: What is Earth's deepest known oceanic point?
geo-016.statement: The Mariana Trench is so deep that Mount Everest could fit inside it with over a mile of water to spare.

geo-017.quizQuestion: Why is Antarctica classified as a desert?
geo-017.statement: Antarctica is technically a desert — it receives less annual precipitation than the Sahara.

geo-018.quizQuestion: Which international land border is longest?
geo-018.statement: The border between the USA and Canada is the longest international border in the world, stretching 8,891 kilometers.

geo-019.quizQuestion: What percentage of Greenland is covered by ice?
geo-019.statement: Greenland is the world's largest island, but it belongs to Denmark — a country 50 times smaller.

geo-020.quizQuestion: What causes the Tropics to shift toward the equator?
geo-020.statement: The Tropic of Capricorn and the Tropic of Cancer are slowly moving — each shifts about 15 meters closer to the equator every year.

tech-001.quizQuestion: What insect was found in the Harvard Mark II computer?
tech-001.statement: The first computer bug was a literal insect — a moth found trapped in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947.

tech-002.quizQuestion: What enables one fiber optic strand to carry multiple data channels?
tech-002.statement: A single strand of fiber optic cable can carry more than 1 petabit of data per second — enough to stream every film ever made in under a minute.

tech-003.quizQuestion: How much RAM did Apollo 11's guidance computer have?
tech-003.statement: The Apollo 11 guidance computer that landed humans on the Moon had 4 KB of RAM — about 100,000 times less memory than a modern smartphone.

tech-004.quizQuestion: How much data transmits daily via internet mid-2020s?
tech-004.statement: The internet transmits approximately 5 exabytes of data every single day — more data than was created in all of human history up to the year 2003.

tech-005.quizQuestion: What inspired George de Mestral to invent Velcro?
tech-005.statement: Velcro was invented in 1941 after Swiss engineer George de Mestral returned from a hike and noticed burdock burrs stuck to his dog's fur.

tech-006.quizQuestion: What control technique keeps Atlas robot balanced during acrobatics?
tech-006.statement: Boston Dynamics' Atlas robot can perform backflips, parkour, and gymnastics routines with precision that took engineers over a decade to achieve.

tech-007.quizQuestion: Why do LLMs fail basic arithmetic despite massive training?
tech-007.statement: GPT-4 was trained on roughly 1 trillion tokens — about 13 million novels' worth of text — yet still makes basic arithmetic errors.

tech-008.quizQuestion: How was graphene first isolated in laboratory?
tech-008.statement: Graphene is a single layer of carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal lattice and is 200 times stronger than steel yet nearly transparent.

tech-009.quizQuestion: What powers Voyager 1 after 45+ years in space?
tech-009.statement: The Voyager 1 spacecraft, launched in 1977, is now over 23 billion kilometers from Earth and still communicates using a 23-watt radio — about the power of a refrigerator light bulb.

tech-010.quizQuestion: Why did Tim Berners-Lee invent the World Wide Web?
tech-010.statement: The World Wide Web was invented in 1989 by Tim Berners-Lee as a tool to help physicists at CERN share documents — and his boss initially called the proposal 'vague but exciting.'

tech-011.quizQuestion: What quantum property lets qubits represent 0 and 1 simultaneously?
tech-011.statement: A quantum computer solved in 200 seconds a problem that would take the world's fastest classical supercomputer 10,000 years.

tech-012.quizQuestion: Why is JWST's mirror coated in gold?
tech-012.statement: The James Webb Space Telescope's primary mirror is coated in 48.25 grams of gold — less gold than a typical wedding ring — yet collects light from galaxies 13.6 billion light-years away.

tech-013.quizQuestion: Why do EV batteries lose range in cold weather?
tech-013.statement: Lithium-ion batteries powering electric vehicles lose capacity in cold weather because lithium ions move more slowly through the electrolyte at low temperatures.

tech-014.quizQuestion: What enables Hyperloop pods to exceed 1,000 km/h?
tech-014.statement: The Hyperloop concept proposes transporting passengers in pods through near-vacuum tubes at over 1,000 km/h — faster than a commercial airliner cruises.

tech-015.quizQuestion: How many microseconds per day do GPS clocks gain due to relativity?
tech-015.statement: The Global Positioning System (GPS) must account for Einstein's theory of relativity or it would accumulate position errors of about 10 kilometers per day.

tech-016.quizQuestion: Why did Chuck Hull invent 3D printing in 1983?
tech-016.statement: 3D printing technology was invented in 1983 by Chuck Hull, who created it so he could quickly prototype plastic parts for his furniture company.

tech-017.quizQuestion: What steers Falcon 9 during atmospheric reentry landing?
tech-017.statement: SpaceX's Falcon 9 booster lands itself upright after launch using three landing legs and throttleable rocket engines, a feat engineers once considered physically impossible.

tech-018.quizQuestion: How do wind turbine blades generate rotational force?
tech-018.statement: Wind turbines use aerodynamic blades — not sails — and the tips of large turbine blades can travel at over 320 km/h even when the hub appears to turn slowly.

tech-019.quizQuestion: How many interlocking gears did the Antikythera mechanism contain?
tech-019.statement: The Antikythera mechanism, found in a shipwreck off Greece and dated to around 100 BCE, is an ancient analog computer that predicted astronomical events and eclipses.

tech-020.quizQuestion: CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing was inspired by what?
tech-020.statement: CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing technology was inspired by the natural immune system of bacteria, which store snippets of viral DNA to recognize and destroy invaders.

cult-001.quizQuestion: Why does the Mona Lisa lack eyebrows?
cult-001.statement: The Mona Lisa has no eyebrows — Renaissance Florentine fashion dictated that shaved eyebrows were the height of elegance.

cult-002.quizQuestion: What condition did Beethoven have composing his Ninth Symphony?
cult-002.statement: Beethoven composed his Ninth Symphony — including the famous 'Ode to Joy' — after he was completely deaf.

cult-003.quizQuestion: What could different musical modes change in ancient Greeks?
cult-003.statement: The ancient Greeks believed music was so powerful that different musical modes could literally change a person's moral character.

cult-004.quizQuestion: What did Odin sacrifice for wisdom from the Well?
cult-004.statement: The Norse god Odin sacrificed one of his eyes to gain wisdom, then hung himself from a tree for nine days to learn the secret of writing.

cult-005.quizQuestion: Why do shrimp symbolize longevity in Osechi Ryori?
cult-005.correctAnswer: They curl like elderly people's backs
cult-005.statement: Japan's 'Osechi Ryori' New Year food tradition assigns specific meanings to every dish — black beans represent health, herring roe represents fertility, and shrimp represent longevity because they curl like an old person's back.

cult-006.quizQuestion: What saved the Eiffel Tower from 1909 demolition?
cult-006.statement: The Eiffel Tower was almost torn down in 1909 and was only saved because it was useful as a giant radio antenna.

cult-007.quizQuestion: What volcanic event inspired Mary Shelley to write Frankenstein?
cult-007.correctAnswer: Mount Tambora eruption caused cold summer
cult-007.statement: Mary Shelley wrote Frankenstein at age 18 during a ghost story contest on a stormy night — and invented the science fiction genre in the process.

cult-008.quizQuestion: How did Aztecs originally consume chocolate?
cult-008.statement: Chocolate was originally consumed exclusively as a bitter, spicy drink — adding sugar was a European innovation that would have horrified the Aztecs who invented it.

cult-009.quizQuestion: Who operated the Colosseum's retractable fabric roof?
cult-009.statement: The ancient Colosseum in Rome had a retractable fabric roof called the 'velarium' operated by sailors from the Roman navy.

cult-010.quizQuestion: What film subject reportedly terrified the first paying audience?
cult-010.statement: The first film ever shown to a paying audience was less than one minute long and depicted workers leaving a factory — and the audience reportedly ran screaming from the theater.

cult-011.quizQuestion: What rule did ancient Olympics create after discovering a disguised woman?
cult-011.correctAnswer: Trainers appear naked to prove male
cult-011.statement: The ancient Olympics banned married women from attending — but a widow named Kallipateira disguised herself as a trainer to watch her son compete, was discovered, and the rules were immediately changed.

cult-012.quizQuestion: What colors did medieval European peasants typically wear?
cult-012.statement: Medieval European peasants wore bright, vivid colors — the idea that medieval people dressed in drab browns and grays is a Hollywood myth.

cult-013.quizQuestion: Why did Shah Jahan commission the Taj Mahal?
cult-013.statement: The Taj Mahal took 22 years to build and employed over 20,000 workers — yet Mughal Emperor Shah Jahan reportedly had the lead architect's hands cut off to prevent him building anything more beautiful.

cult-014.quizQuestion: How many English words did Shakespeare invent that we still use?
cult-014.statement: Shakespeare invented over 1,700 words that we still use today, including 'bedroom,' 'lonely,' 'generous,' 'obscene,' and 'critic.'

cult-015.quizQuestion: Why did wealthy Egyptians shave heads then wear wigs?
cult-015.statement: The ancient Egyptians wore wigs — and the quality and style of your wig indicated your social status, with the wealthiest wearing heavy perfumed wigs made from human hair.

cult-016.quizQuestion: Why did Homer describe the sea as 'wine-dark' instead of blue?
cult-016.correctAnswer: Ancient Greek lacked a word for blue
cult-016.statement: The ancient Greeks had no word for the color blue — and Homer's Odyssey describes the sea as 'wine-dark' because his culture didn't distinguish blue as a separate color category.

cult-017.quizQuestion: What did George Lucas sacrifice for Star Wars sequel rights?
cult-017.statement: The 'Star Wars' franchise was rejected by every major Hollywood studio before 20th Century Fox agreed to produce it — and George Lucas gave up his director's fee in exchange for the sequel rights.

cult-018.quizQuestion: How long is the Mahabharata compared to Iliad and Odyssey?
cult-018.statement: The ancient Indian epic Mahabharata is the longest poem ever written — at 1.8 million words, it is ten times the length of the Iliad and Odyssey combined.

cult-019.quizQuestion: What ancient Greek ritual inspired the birthday candle tradition?
cult-019.correctAnswer: Offerings to Artemis at her temple
cult-019.statement: The tradition of blowing out birthday candles originated in ancient Greece, where people brought offerings of moon-shaped honey cakes lit with candles to the temple of Artemis.

cult-020.quizQuestion: Why were high heels originally invented?
cult-020.correctAnswer: To grip stirrups while shooting arrows
cult-020.statement: High heels were originally invented for men — specifically for Persian and European male cavalry soldiers who needed heels to lock their feet in stirrups while shooting arrows on horseback.

genet-victoriae-001.quizQuestion: What is a giant forest genet?
genet-victoriae-001.statement: Giant forest genets are small carnivorous mammals found in African forests.

parnassius-apollo-001.quizQuestion: What type of animal is Parnassius apollo?
parnassius-apollo-001.statement: Parnassius apollo is a butterfly species found in European mountain regions.

senna-alata-001.statement: Senna alata is a tropical plant species with least concern conservation status.

leopard-cat-001.quizQuestion: What is the scientific name of the leopard cat?
leopard-cat-001.statement: Leopard cats are small wild felines native to Asia with spotted coats.

phaseolus-vulgaris-001.quizQuestion: What is Phaseolus vulgaris commonly known as?
phaseolus-vulgaris-001.statement: Phaseolus vulgaris is a legume crop cultivated worldwide for food.

serval-001.quizQuestion: What African wild cat has exceptionally long legs?
serval-001.statement: Servals are African wild cats with distinctive long legs and spotted coats.

laetacara-curviceps-001.statement: Laetacara curviceps is a small freshwater fish species of least concern.

baikal-seal-001.quizQuestion: What is the Baikal seal's primary habitat?
baikal-seal-001.statement: Baikal seals are the only freshwater seal species in the world.

alpine-pika-001.quizQuestion: What family do Alpine pikas belong to?
alpine-pika-001.statement: Alpine pikas are small mammals in the Ochotonidae family found in mountainous regions.

red-fox-001.quizQuestion: What is the scientific name for red foxes?
red-fox-001.statement: Red foxes are highly adaptable carnivores found across diverse habitats worldwide.

mullus-barbatus-001.statement: Mullus barbatus is a red mullet fish species with least concern conservation status.

nannacara_aureocephalus_001.statement: Nannacara aureocephalus is a small fish species with least concern conservation status.

pomegranate-001.quizQuestion: What type of plant is a pomegranate?
pomegranate-001.statement: Pomegranate is a fruit-bearing deciduous shrub native to regions.

anim-wolverine-001.quizQuestion: What type of animal is the Wolverine?
anim-wolverine-001.statement: The Wolverine (Gulo gulo) is classified as Least Concern.

anim-house-sparrow-002.quizQuestion: What type of animal is the House Sparrow?
anim-house-sparrow-002.statement: The House Sparrow (Passer domesticus) is classified as Least Concern.

anim-mallard-003.quizQuestion: What type of animal is the Mallard?
anim-mallard-003.statement: The Mallard (Anas platyrhynchos) is classified as Least Concern.

anim-eurasian-jay-004.quizQuestion: What type of animal is the Eurasian Jay?
anim-eurasian-jay-004.statement: The Eurasian Jay (Garrulus glandarius) is classified as Least Concern.

anim-white-stork-005.quizQuestion: What type of animal is the White Stork?
anim-white-stork-005.statement: The White Stork (Ciconia ciconia) is classified as Least Concern.

anim-accipiter-gentilis-006.quizQuestion: What type of animal is the Accipiter gentilis?
anim-accipiter-gentilis-006.statement: The Accipiter gentilis (Accipiter gentilis) is classified as Least Concern.

anim-northern-raven-007.quizQuestion: What type of animal is the Northern Raven?
anim-northern-raven-007.statement: The Northern Raven (Corvus corax) is classified as Least Concern.

anim-european-nightjar-008.quizQuestion: What type of animal is the European Nightjar?
anim-european-nightjar-008.statement: The European Nightjar (Caprimulgus europaeus) is classified as Least Concern.

anim-canada-goose-009.quizQuestion: What type of animal is the Canada Goose?
anim-canada-goose-009.statement: The Canada Goose (Branta canadensis) is classified as Least Concern.

anim-black-redstart-010.quizQuestion: What type of animal is the Black Redstart?
anim-black-redstart-010.statement: The Black Redstart (Phoenicurus ochruros) is classified as Least Concern.

anim-arctic-tern-011.quizQuestion: What type of animal is the Arctic Tern?
anim-arctic-tern-011.statement: The Arctic Tern (Sterna paradisaea) is classified as Least Concern.

anim-common-ringed-plover-012.quizQuestion: What type of animal is the Common Ringed Plover?
anim-common-ringed-plover-012.statement: The Common Ringed Plover (Charadrius hiaticula) is classified as Least Concern.

anim-atlantic-emperor-013.quizQuestion: What type of animal is the Atlantic emperor?
anim-atlantic-emperor-013.statement: The Atlantic emperor (Lethrinus atlanticus) is classified as Least Concern.

anim-lethrinus-reticulatus-014.quizQuestion: What type of animal is the Lethrinus reticulatus?
anim-lethrinus-reticulatus-014.statement: The Lethrinus reticulatus (Lethrinus reticulatus) is classified as Least Concern.

anim-lethrinus-enigmaticus-015.quizQuestion: What type of animal is the Lethrinus enigmaticus?
anim-lethrinus-enigmaticus-015.statement: The Lethrinus enigmaticus (Lethrinus enigmaticus) is classified as Least Concern.
